All right, so let me let me stop by this and say that You know the word brand of what here so the best way for me to kind of explain to you why most people struggle so much when it comes to making a Repeatable sales presentation others the key is that every once in a while Yeah, if you're really in state and you kind of feeling really good and it stars a line and you get off this perfect sales Presentation where you sound great. You look great. You know everything is in the right order and then we end up closing the deal Right, but for most people that's the exception to the rule. It's not the rule most of the time their Presentation is you know, basically like let's say this is a 10 your best Presentation and your average presentation like you have seven or six or eight, right? You don't get that perfect presentation every single time and I will tell you that when you do This is not gonna be the one right? We make a shit presentation after a while You give any experience or you can deliver an okay presentation, but the question is is how do you get to this spot? Right here. Well, literally every sales presentation is close to your best and The way to do that is through the use of what we call strips and Scripps are probably most misunderstood word up there with rapport in the whole sales vernacular about what a Script and how does it fit the overall sales process? See the bottom lines are so many people out there that they flinch or they recoil at the idea of Use a script. That's so unauthentic and you know, okay I'm gonna sound wooden my clothes may will drop people will know I'm using a script And let me just tell you the bottom line. You're gonna wipe that shit out of your head Let me give you the best example You know the movie brave heart I'm sure you've seen it right with Mel Gibson remember that scene in brave heart when bother if you have the media serve up I right now be playing it in the background right? But that's next week right so the scene from brave hot where Mel Gibson He's got a little blue paint on his face right? He rides up on his horse and he sees all those people at the Scottish people like a rag tag group of Scots, right? It's like then makeshift army them Alicia, right? And they're on one side of the field then on the other side of the field is there, you know 10 times or five times as many English redcoats perfectly formed right with their horses narrow archers and everything and you know And then the whole my yards right it just perfectly in perfect formation and they're about to slaughter the Scots They're just heavily outmatched and then Mel Gibson rides up and he gives this in power speech And he says sons of Scotland I stand here in the face of 500 years of rape and murder and tyranny and prep lava and he gives this Unbelievable rousing speech in the Scots go fucking wild He motivates the hot of them and he goes let's get him and they fucking go and they fucking wipe out the superior English army Right, I'm sure you've seen that scene so an amazing scene blood splattering everywhere He needs the camera lens once my favorite part right amazing scene so let me ask you a question about that See I'm sure if you're like me you all like really moved emotionally by that and you fucking which is so into it's like It was a rousing speech, right? Let me ask you a question you think all Mel winged it Do you think Mel Gibson kind of just made that up at the last second? Do you think the producer the director said okay now listen Mel? I'm gonna line up this thousand extras here And then they have like three thousand people on the other side and we're gonna wait just the right time of the day With a son a setting and the lighting is perfect And I want you to write up all you also put blue shit on your face right to make you look really fierce and tough and then Just go out there and just fucking motivate them now. Just go get him going just you know just rock and roll Mel You think that's what the director did mean God knows what would come out of Mel Gibson's mouth back in the day Right when he was drunk all time right he's recovering since then but he's a odd mother fuckers gotta who knows what he would have said But they don't do that Instead they hand Mel Gibson a script that was written by a professional who spent countless hours on this one scene Preparing for this one scene and he mapped out exactly in every words What's the best thing we can say how it should we structure that so it comes to a crescendo right the end so everyone's so motivated and Mel Gibson the actor mastered the art of reading from a script Without sounding like he's reading from the script and that's the key in other words actors and salespeople who are using the straight line You master the art of reading from a script without Sounding like you're reading from a script now also was Mel sitting there with your script like this sons of Scotland No, okay, so if you're selling people say oh, I sell face-to-face. I can't use a script bullshit Number one you can memorize whatever it is you need to memorize and two when you're selling face-to-face It's not so much that you have a strip that says every single word in order But you have your core language patterns. In other words, you're gonna have three core patterns that Scribe your product the features and the benefits most importantly in the most coherent perfect way In other words, they're structured Beautifully and perfectly and exactly the right over you're gonna have three things already written about you yourself, right? The second of the three 10s, how do you sell yourself? You're gonna have that written in next year. How do you so resell the company? How do you ask for the order the right way? How do you lay out each aspect of the sale in perfect fashion? It is all created beforehand and the straight line terminology for that is called strategic Preparation Strategic preparation and what strategic preparation is It's a philosophy of Preparedness that almost borders on over preparedness does it's imagine anticipating every single possible outcome that could happen in the sale Before you walk into something's walking in the customer shoes imagining how they're gonna react to everything you say what their questions will be what their objections will be what would you want the year view of the customer and actually taking all of that beforehand and essentially Mapping that into a template of a series of scripts That's just one series of scripts that work together To move your prospect to an incredibly high level of certainty both logically and emotionally That's what a great script does and just so you know it's not just brave heart Every single movie that you've watched every TV series that you connected to that caused you to binge watch We just want to keep watching they hooked you in both logically and emotionally they got you emotionally invested in the characters Guess what every single one was for a script They all use scripts you're watching and being moved by scripted language Scripted prose and here's the irony when they you know first thought of it this um, you know so-called real TV, right? You know where it's like, you know basically reality TV, right? And they said okay, this is great. You don't even have to have a writer We can just have the people talk you know what they found and unless they actually gave them fucking scripts It was so fucking boring no one wanted to watch so they started telling them what to do having them you get it they actually something giving them things to say and Guiding the process so even reality TV in the absence of scripts. It's boring and no one connects with it So essentially what a script now take does that make sense to everyone everyone say yes that makes sense press press y Okay, I want to make sure everyone gets this because it's a fundamental concept because what happens is This is weird Like kind of energy out there about oh scripts is evil scripts is inauthentic and if I use a script outside It's just not true Every great sales person is using a script whether they know it or not Great natural porn clothes is that haven't actually written out of their brain is wired away Where they're saying the same things every time their brain just does to them automatically What we do with the straight line Is we take that process and we formalize it? So at the end of the day If you make 100 presentations They're all up here in this 10 area 9.8 No, some days you'll have a little bit of tone out like this But you get yourself to the point where you start sounding awesome every single time And when you get there your closing rate is going to soar So that's the first thing basically shut my phone off here. Okay Okay, that says like the first thing here is that basically you must knock out Any negative anchor you have towards using scripts They're an integral part of the straight line and without them people tend to just fucking flop and flounder And they never get to the highest level of sale. Okay Make sense Good. All right. Now. Let me turn the page here and tell you what The strip actually is now that's that That's a deeper now a script Basically represents the well thought out essence of the perfect sale It's the well thought out essence of the perfect sale So imagine if you could go back in time to the 10 best sales presentations that you ever made for a particular product that you're selling And you take those and you'll line it all but you extract that out and say which of these 10 When did I sound the best in the introduction right or in the opening? When did I describe this aspect the best and you take all 10 of those? And you put them side by side you extract out the very best language patterns from all of them And you essentially roll them up into one cohesive end-to-end script That allows you to close anybody who's closeable. That's what a script is. So it's it's essentially the amalgamation of your 10 best presentations of all time Where you talked about yourself in the best way where you talked about your product in the best way you talk about the company You did your energy in benefits out equation And by the way for those who don't know what that is you don't own the you don't own the straight line system You should get it. Okay, but the point is I'll explain now briefly Okay, because it's such an integral part of closing the deal of energy in benefits So all if you don't know is I'll explain in a minute. Okay, but the point is that's what your script is It's that well thought out essence of the perfect sale You're lining up all your scripts side by side In fact what I do when I do my consulting and most of my work is consulting with companies By the way, that's what I just told me. It's one of my clients, right? Is the way you have a company develop scripts Is you have the salesman break down into groups and you have each salesperson say I want you to take all your best presentations and I want you to pick out the three best language patterns That you have ever uttered. I just like accident on purpose and ever utter about a product What's the three best ways you could explain your product and I want you to write those down in your product section They don't want you to write down the three best patterns you've ever uttered about yourself In the same thing with the three best patterns about the company, right? Then I want you to write out your three best looping patterns And I have them all write this down and they get together in a group and they go through it They mix and they match and what they end up coming out with is this beautiful end-to-end Sort out of your mouth and goes all the way down to the close and includes the looping patterns and everything else That's what a great script does. Okay, so watch now. Let me start by drawing straight Why don't we give you that one of one like about a 30 minute? Like you know sprint here that's going to show you exactly how you essentially use the plug-and-play Templates of the straight line to structure your scripts. So remember it comes down to you You're taking all your best presentations and hopefully you're in a group of two or three salespeople and you're collapsing them into this one Perfect presentation that goes end to end. All right, so let me start like this I told you I did a whiteboard Wednesday and I spoke about this insanity about the new model of selling Versus the old model. I said the new model of selling is this sort of consultative model where you spend 40 percent of your time They call it essentially building relationships, right building relationships, right that you spend 30 percent of your time Okay, and they they call that um gathering identifying needs Identifying needs instead of 20 percent of your time offering solutions and they spend 10 percent of your time confirming and closing That's consultative selling and it's fucking idiotically ridiculous because let me just tell you flat out If you spend 10 percent of your time closing you're going to be broke. You'll never close anyone. That's not the way it works I'll explain why in a second Same but the same token if you spend only 20 percent of your time offering a solution You're broke too Okay, and I'm going to loop back and explain why now let's go to the old one Which is more of your hard charging model where they say you spend 10 percent of your time getting to report 20 percent of your time qualifying your prospect 30 percent of your time Making your presentation and then 40 percent of your time closing and overcoming objections So they in the old models like that hard charging s for the order s for the order 40 here's the deal They're both bullshit. They're both idiotically ridiculous. Why because where you are selling Effectively at the highest level using the straight line and closing anyone is closeable. That's what happens when you sell effectively You're doing all these things at the same time You're always closing from the first word out of your mouth. You're closing the prospect that you're a person worth listening to When you're asking questions you're closing them on the fact they should answer your questions Fourth right of it. You're an expert in your field that makes sense to them to take the risk of opening up for you You're always doing all these things at once and especially in this context. You're always Presenting you're always offering a solution meaning from the first word out of your mouth That's when your sales presentation starts. It's not like we have this whole thing. Okay. Well, let's watch. We'll draw the straight line. I'll show you It's just straight line. This is your open. This is your close open Close your boundaries above and below the line, right? Here's where you in control the sale Here you're out of control for sale Right to know your adage right in control, right? Yeah, these arrows. This represents the perfect sound Right when you're inside these boundaries, you're doing two things You're gathering massive intelligence by asking questions and you're building rapport, right? Now let me very quickly go through the syntax of the straight line. It starts with the first four seconds those first four seconds you must capture the prospect's attention and position yourself as being sharp as attack Enthusiastic a cell and an expert in your field that has to happen in the first four seconds Why because what this shows up to what that means in the mind of the prospect is they say to themselves This is a person worth listening to They say that you're worthless to this is a person that can help me achieve my goals Help me get what they want because they're sharp. They're on the ball They're enthusiastic and they're experts in their field. So they defer to you Right that has to happen in the first or second that opens you up to the next step because they Perceive you as being an expert. They defer to you and they allow you To ask them questions. So you start asking questions That's called gathering intelligence and you're identifying your needs their values their beliefs their pain points And what's their biggest pain point of all right? That's what you're doing All those things when you gather intelligence and at the same time you're doing it the way you're asking your question It's not like a brand inquisitor. You're asking was the trusted friend the doctor the advisor and you listen actively Uh-huh. It's right. It's real lastly right, huh? Yep. Oh, got it your active listening. So that allows you To build that massively tight rapport by that active listening and then the end of the intelligence gathering you have your transition Will you say well, you know, Jim, Jim based on what you said to me this practice a perfect fit for you Let me tell you why that's your transition, right? And if they don't qualify or you know, you can't help them We're going to remove them out of the pipeline because you want to make full blow presentations to people who aren't qualified, right? And then from here we transition to the main body of our presentation the main body Right here. This is the main body. We say, okay great name with the company name with the product name with the program Let me give them the exact Benefits the features of something we have as airtight logical case that we're building about the product and at the end We always ask for the order for the first time We always end up presentation by asking for the order for the first time And then based on the response. There's three responses. They could say yes Let's do it. She's a lay down sale. They could say no means they're not interested very rare Usually they say maybe let me think about it. Let me call you back You get hit with a common objection right here and that starts your looping family loop back and represent That's a straight line sale, right? And we've been through this over the weeks and we keep reinforcing the stuff and going through other aspects as well each week, right? Here's my question With the old model of selling or the new model or one They would have you think that your script basically Starts when I make my presentation. I have my full presentation right here. So I already Have to do their attention. I've asked questions I've gotten into rapport. I've done my transition and now I'm going to sell them my product and this is what happens Over the world everywhere. This is the biggest mistake is that sales forces around the world think That their presentation starts when they get to this point the solution It's okay. Great. Now. I've gotten through all this stuff and now we're ready to handle that my product Nothing could be further from the truth Your presentation a straight line sales presentation starts here From the first word out of your mouth Especially from the first word out of your mouth should be scripted You know what you're going to say before you say let me just tell you something in the absence of that If you go and I've done this product say it's almost funny It's almost comical that if you go into an untrained sales force And you watch 40 sales people Try to open them. What's the first word they say those first 10 seconds They say everyone says something different. It's pure fucking nonsense. Most of it Most of them shoot themselves in the foot in the first 10 seconds And then after that when they start to ask questions It's not scripted out and they by the time they're done they poison the sales so badly They have no shot of even making a real presentation. They're not in rapport They're not being perceived as an expert and they're done before they start. It's all before they started You get that in other words The key to a straight line sales presentation Is it doesn't start here when you make your formal presentation on the product? It starts from the first word out of your mouth So what I want you to do not right now because we only have an hour But I want you to do this weekend here as I want you now to actually take the time And if you can get into a group of people that work with you in the same industry in the same company, right? I want you to actually Say, okay. I've probably given you know 500 to 1000 Opens and since I've been selling it'd be even 10,000, right? What's the best way for me? Just to open up my mouth and introduce myself. What? When have I just sounded what was a time I just sounded awesome? Write down three or four different variations of how you think you should really Introduce yourself over those first two seconds. How do you say? Hey, you know, my hands on the belt I work for xyc company wearing this You're so called elevator pitch. Okay, and again, I can't get into when we have the interactive Media I'll have to have someone maybe we'll do it a few weeks. We have to rebuild for other stuff I'll have someone actually go through this with we all choose someone we can do it together But just understand this it's whatever your industry is. I want you to just come What's the best way for you to start to sell and write it down? Go back and say cheese. I've done this 100 times or 1000s write down your three best opens ever And then I want you to merge those into one perfect open And then at the end of that so when you open you say the name of the company said xyz, right? I work so so and let me just ask you a couple of quick questions So I don't waste your time. So what does that mean that point here? And I'm gonna go actually start going through a list for this I want to give you the broad strokes right in other words, you know I say the plug and play template to the straight line in your Builder kit and in your script building section of the actual straight line itself You'll notice that there's these templates there that shows you okay So my open is going to look like one of these three things I'm gonna say something along these lines and I know my open has an ending point Which is going to end where I start asking questions. I say listen Will you say something if you have a minute like ash some questions? I want to find out something to best serve you you got a minute and they say sure Right that marks the end Of your open and from there you're going to transition into your questions You start asking questions now. There's two ways to do this one way The novices way is to fucking wing it in other words You know you have to ask certain things a certain intelligence you have to gather And you're hoping pray That you're in a great state You're in flow and then you end up asking all the questions that you need to ask in exactly the right order You hope that happens and the chances are fucking slim and nil that it actually will happen And as they say some left down a long time ago The point is the chance of you actually asking every question that you have to ask are very low And especially in the right order and in the right tonality See what I want you to do is when it comes to this part of the presentation I will you know strip down all your questions in advance I want you to write down all the go back to your pet 10 best sales presentations and say, okay What are the greatest one of the questions I have to ask and what's the best order to ask them in And in what tonality should I be asking and memorize it if you're over the phone You could have the glide in front of you if you're a person memorize it, okay And then by doing that you know, you're always going to ask the right questions in the right order and also Because you've done it beforehand It now frees up your conscious mind To focus on what the person is saying back to you their body language How they're interpreting your words in other words if you're if you haven't prepared and written down your scripts in advance What happens is you spend so much of your mental energy your conscious mind Is tied up In trying to actually remember everything that you have no Processing power left to actually analyze your prospects body language listen to their tonality So you you basically are selling yourself so short you can't get to the nitty gritty Which is finding out how someone else's feeling and then mirroring that back and matching that back It's so crucial. So again the same way that you Start with the first four seconds. I want you to map out exactly how you're going to ask questions What questions are you going to ask? What order are you going to ask them in? What's the best tonality? To ask each question in you got to know that beforehand and that's part of your script. That's including your script. So on some level A straight line script isn't just one scripts. It's a series of interlocking scripts Is a script for your opening a script for your qualifying your intelligence gallery Is a script for your for your transition a script for your presentation a script for your asking for the order or script There's a series of scripts. They all lock together into one beautiful end to end pattern that allows you to sound your very best Every single time now, of course at the end here when you do your transition. That's when you get into the formal Main body and the mistake most people make here is this is the only part that they make an attempt to script out They they'll actually just give all you know all your mirror and they just write that and they leave everything else to chance And they just fuck it up Don't do that Don't just say you know i'm writing a script that includes me just talking about my product It's end to end. That's the power and the beauty of the straight line Okay, and same thing goes for when you ask for the order for the first time And in your looping patterns, okay? So that's essentially the big picture. Okay, so let's see what time is Perfect. So about 30 minutes in now what I want to do is I want us like getting I have a list of things actually I've actually mapped this out for you. So I have the whole list here of all the points I need to hit so I'm going to go through them one by one now to make sure you get everything you need. Okay Okay, so I just uh, yeah take this moment Stop start my client. So nobody pays us. It's what the fuck's going on. All right, all right here are the Basically the elements of a grade script and there's 10 of them overall these are 10 essentially rules You have to abide by okay, so number one the main Body of your script should be no more than one page When it's written out meaning you don't want to front load. This is the the biggest mistake that people make is they Frontload their scripts. So they put all the benefits. So imagine watch imagine that you Go through these first few steps here, right? Will you Make your first four seconds perfect You capture their attention To proceed the right way as an expert shall attack enthusiastic You then use that positioning to start asking some really great questions And as you ask those questions, you ask them in a certain way and the tonality that allows you to build massive rapport Especially because when they answer you like, uh-huh Yep, uh-huh. That's your active listening. So by doing that, right You end up when you're asked your last question. You should be in such Tight rapport with the prospect They should be saying wow this guy's really shocked this girl's really sure she really knows her stuff She seems to really care about me. She's not like he's not every other salesperson This one's that's what you want them thinking And the way you do that is by asking smart questions in the right order using the right tonality and engage your active listening And then when you ask all the questions you need to ask Right And you know, which means that you now know everything that you need to know To decide whether or not the person can afford your product Whether they can benefit from your product or they actually need your product Right and decide whether they should keep going down the straight line In other words, if you can't help someone or they can't afford your product, right? They're a cleric or a lookie-loo. You're not going to want to spend 10 15 minutes And then making a full blown presentation and even more than on looping you're wasting your time So you want to exit those people out of the system so you can have a pattern for doing that but for those people that actually Qualify remember we went through this last week with our bias and our and buyers and heat right if those of you who knew I'm actually you could go back. I'm archiving the last month always any given time you'll have the last month of trainings, right? So go back to last week's training. You get it's getting this part of the package because you signed up, right? So you'll see about who actually goes down the straight line and who doesn't who gets eliminated from the pipeline So point being is what is your script right here and your transition? Well, it's going to be something along the lines of you know, jim Jim Based on everything you just said to me This program is a perfect fit you This home This insurance policy This tv is the best one. This car is the best one for you. This car means whatever you're selling your transitions very simple Jim jill based on what You said to me I asked you questions. You gave me answers based on that This is a perfect fit this one not anyone this one That's what an expert does remember that the doctor the doctor asks you questions And he kind of laser guides in on what's wrong with you and then when he's actually asked everything he knows what's wrong He makes his prognosis. So essentially this this transition here is your Period we actually start saying and here's my prognosis based on the questions like asha. Here's what's wrong He has a solution to your pain. That's what a doctor does and that's what you're going to do as a salesperson so Let me tell you why That sets you up now for the main body of your presentation And the mistake that people make here Is they tend to throw the fucking kitchen sink They throw the kitchen sink at this they tell you say everything Don't do that. You don't want to have a main presentation That's a mile long that lasts for 30 minutes because what happens is people get overwhelmed and also you can't Take someone to an ultra high level of certainty in one shot now remember like you know what happens is you move someone down the straight line Okay, hold on a second here As you move someone down the straight line, right? What you're doing is you're raising up their level of certainty They might start like they might start at four or five But by the time you're done everything you say everything you do is designed to increase their level of certainty massive That's what you're doing, right? Well, guess what? You can't take someone from a fourth to a tenth in one shot That's not how human beings are wired. We get certain in Doses and stages so you might take someone from a four to seven and they back off to a six See it so they start off at a four they're like, yeah, it sounds pretty good Then you say something really compelling like oh wow that sounds real. I get that's great And then as and then 10 seconds later they're like slightly less certain certainty wanes It's like almost like a race car. You know when your car goes And so it goes up as high as it can Then you shift and there's a pause and it drops a bit in speed and intensity and then takes off to a higher level Your pause it drops in intensity. That's how You get it. That's how people become certain in stages So what happens is if you just go out there and front load Meaning you just fucking unleash everything right up front You got nothing left to say a you overwhelm the prospect and then the worst things when they say let me think about it You have nothing left to say to them nothing compelling already said everything compelling So save some of your key benefits for later on in your looping patterns You want to get them certainly want to essentially frame But not front load frame your airtight logical case. That's number one. Okay. Let me just check on so yeah Okay, perfect now next number two Each word is crucial in other words, the the biggest problem With most scripts that aren't straight line scripts is that they are not taken to a point Where they sound so good. That's so smooth as silk that they're actually usable by a salesperson in other words straight line script Actually is usable. It's been meticulously gone through So every single word has the right number of syllables and beats and there's no tongue twisters when it's you actually have gone through it And each word is fucking perfect because here's what happens If you have a script and it's full of tongue twisters and difficult things to say It becomes unusable and it starts to sound Like someone's reading from the script and that is the death in salesperson So the reason people have negative anchors if we want to really look at this logically the reason people Dislike scripts. It's because they have to see a good one If you have a great script, it's like fucking magic. It's like perfectly smooth You sound perfectly natural You get it a shitty script is unusable because if you don't sound natural then you can't use it So again, each word is crucial. Make sure you read through it again and again Illuminate a tongue twisters. Make sure the right thing is a rhythm and a flow. It's got to sound almost poetic almost musical Okay, and for those of you have the straight line system that go through this and nausea in the actual lower training. Okay, number three Check in with the prospect often Meaning you don't want to Go through this and just go And go from a to z and then never say anything to the prospect just keep giving them information information You want to give them a chunk of information say make sense to you got it so far you follow me so far. They say yep great Check in with your prospect every 30 seconds or so Make sure they're with you make sure they haven't tuned out make sure they're not getting over and if they're like saying I don't really get that stop Loop back and then go again and make sure that they are with you Okay, it's key. You have to check in with the prospect. You can't just talk once you start talking You're a talking head and you're closing like plummets to the floor. Okay number four Use and I'll by the way, I'm gonna put this slide right in front Just there's a reason why I don't have it up because with this filming it doesn't look good on camera That's why I really said have the power point slides up next week the media server This will all be up. It'll be really elegant. You love it. So I'll just show this to the camera So you can write it down real quick. Let's take a snapshot or a screenshot. Okay? Or if you don't need to because actually it's on the uh, science tape, right? Okay, so number four use transitional words and phrases things like now No, w well, so I say now now john. I don't say John I say now john Well, the reason for the call you have these little words and they act as transitional words now Well, the cause so and the reason you use those words is because they give you a chance to pause And change your tonality and keep your beats Correct, keep the rhythm correct. So remember your traditional transitional ones Don't just blow through the whole thing without having any well now. However Now the reason for the call so we never just say someone the reason for the call I say now The reason for the call and that word now Gives me that time to catch my breath Drop my voice. So I say it does this this now the reason for the call Now is your transitional word. So remember your transitional word. So, um A powerful picture, right? Um, what does that mean? That means whenever possible we want to sell through metaphors and stories and similes for instance One of the greatest scripts I ever wrote was about Eastman Kodak, right? And I would say, you know Eastman was a stock It's nothing anymore, right because of you know the changes in photography back in the 80s This was the greatest fucking stock play ever They've been sued by Polaroid for patent infringement and the stock had gone from like 75 down to 30 And the thesis the investment thesis was that when the litigation gets settled, right? The stock will go flying back up, right? So the so you would say, you know, listen Kodak based on its book value right now if you take the company break it up It's 120 hours right now. It's trading at 35 Right based on its earnings every company in the consumer products Who trades 15 times earnings earning $5 a share through the math five times 15 75 the stock is 30 So that's how you make sort of a basic Non-story not painting a picture. That's just fact fact fact, right? Rather you'd say remember when that plant exploded in bullpile India back in the early 80s And then 15,000 Indians were killed. Well, the day the plant exploded the stock dropped from 45 down to three Guess what when litigation was finally settled five years later the stock ran back from three to 72 You you um us steel got sued by nipon steel the day that us still got sued for antitrust The stock dropped from 46 amiguris. I'm talking down to nine Dave litigation settled the stock ran to 65 and yet texaco, which is sued by penns oil Stock was 84 the day the lawsuit was filed the stock dropped to 16 Two years later. They settled the suit the stock ran to 104 Let me ask you a question right now. Kodak is a $30 a share It wasn't 75 before the lawsuit was filed They have a billion dollars aside and set aside their value to pay for any damages Where do you think the price is going after they settle litigation with polaroid and all the institutions was pouring back into the stock? Where do you think it's going? They'll say oh probably a lot eggs. That's telling a story You'd sell by metaphors in comparisons So look to compare your product to something else in the past That's a story you sell by comparison by metaphors very powerful stuff. Okay next, okay? Link your goods or services to a trustworthy figure This is one of the most important things that you can do whatever possible embedded in your scripts in your patterns I want you to have saying this company uses it as well named some big blue chip companies some famous individuals Like, you know warren buffett believes in this strategy He's using it himself the biggest firms around you know, you know the Ferrari racing team is using this particular add-on for a car salesman or for insurance. You could say the top insurance whatever use trustworthy figures And famous people and that creates social proofs like using testimonials almost very subtly within a sale It's a very powerful thing to do okay number seven focus on your benefits and not on features now Let me say that again. There's this whole Thing out there and it's kind of stupid about you know Only sell the benefits don't sell the features But just so you know you can't just speak about benefits because if you do you sound like a moron you have to list the feature And then expand on the benefits. You can't just put benefits or else what you're doing is that you're supposed to say What does that think about that? You can't you have to have both the problem is Is that people have a tendency when they write their scripts to make it feature centric? They spend a lot of time explaining the feature And only this much time explaining the benefit. I want you to reverse that dramatically You mention the feature And then you explain all the benefits expound on the benefits. You do that you're in serious business Okay, but you have to mention both. It's like I love some say. Oh, don't talk about the features You have to talk about the features. Okay, just make sure the balance is correct. Okay number eight This is important create urgency at the end What do I mean by that? Well there's two ways For a prospect to be feeling At the end when you ask you they could say they want to buy Which is a great way Imagine like they had done with your presentation you asked for the order and you have your cross feeling Oh, I want to buy this this makes sense. Yeah, I want to do this. That's great What's even better is if at the end of the presentation they say I want to buy now Buy now You need to create urgency at the end of your Presentation and that pushes people to want to buy now. Now what I don't want you to do is create Artificial bogus urgency. That's not ethical But very often you can find some legitimate reasons why The prospect should buy now and if you own your own company or you're working for something speak to the owner but one of the Smart things that marketers do is they actually build urgency into their offers. They put Timeless, okay the offer expires in five days Okay, like the first thing I said today was I said, boh, by the way, I'm raising the price for these trainings That's sure. I am you guys are locked in at this price But tomorrow I'm raising the price to 50 not a lot to 15 14 95 a month Why am I doing that because it's just it's way too cheap. I know it's too cheap to joke Okay, even $15 is way too cheap, but the point I'm looking to go based on volume So now that I have my server up I'm raising that price So today I said to people hey, by the way, I'm raising the price tomorrow that creates urgency If you want to in at the low price buy now or there's only x number of these left There's only 200 seats available And they're going fast. So the key to getting involved in you know In benefiting is you got to sign up now Create urgency at the end if there's not urgency in your offer change your offer Seriously, it's such an important aspect of closing that if there is no urgency then do something to your offer create urgency Put a time limit on it put a certain number then the price goes up create urgency. It's very powerful. It works like a charm. Okay number nine Always end by going into a soft close Meaning you don't want to say to someone John John believe me. You give me one shot. You'll be very very impressed sound good People don't close when they're feeling pressured. They just do not close doesn't work. Okay. You don't want to do that It's not good for anybody. It's not good for the universe. You close John. You give me one shot and believe me You're gonna be very very impressed sound fair enough You close softly using certainty Into sincerity And then the reasonable man sound fair enough sound good I'm reasonable. You're reasonable. Okay. Don't close with pressure. It doesn't work. Okay. And now next year You want to focus on this is a big one here my favorite one. Okay No, I'll ask what's the relationship between sales and marketing? Okay, well that's a pretty basic one. Also, let me give you the answer here so Sales and marketing work hand in hand with each other and represent like the most the active part of any business in other words, you have watch you have Marketing on one side of the equation And you have sales on the other the job of marketing is to go out into the world And first identify who your best buyers Who is your target market? Who are the people most likely to benefit from what i'm selling and then What are all the various ways? And then with the idea that the most cost effective ones one of the most cost effective ways that can reach That is people as possible now from there there could be um an outcome of to get them to walk into my store To click on a link to a website you get it to fill out a form to fill out a coupon Whatever it might be they have to take you the goal of a marketing campaign is to get the prospect to take some action So they then essentially come into the company's sales funnel So online will be to click on this link here and fill out the information And then you send them back to us and now you have their email and their phone numbers You want to get them into your sales funnel? That's the job of marketing. Okay Now there's two types of marketing right this online marketing and this offline marketing online marketing is basically your Google your facebook your instagram linked in right you have your social media aspect of your regular um, you know google the internet right and for that you have seo Which is where you have basically organic searches So this thing you can do to actually make sure that with so many googles for your type of product You come up as high as possible on the page right and you also have pain campaigns a paper click and you're sorry campaign That's for googling of social media We actually go to companies like facebook and instagram and so forth and you're actually Using their database basically and you're really very closely matching the right people It's pretty easy to do on facebook for your product and you get that message out in front of them Hoping that they'll click on a link and you'll get the information and then they go into the sales funnel Then it goes to the sales force and the job of the sales force Is to somehow reach out to those people either by telephone in person some way or by an email Whatever it's going to be and they try to close them to get them to buy something So essentially marketing is the process of getting your message out there Identifying your best buy is getting the message out there And then they go into your funnel and then sales the job of the sales force is to slam it closed and turn that into money One without the other very hard to make a lot of money now Sometimes you find that sales people are forced to do their own walking get L. M. Off it is door to door right? It goes both ways So it's not always divided up and in a good company those functions get divided up at least somewhere okay Oh, good question so I mean the most the most effective way I I've had it's not going in a mirror It's it's been mocked present interesting to people you want to do between people not a mirror It doesn't really work though. I don't like me busy. I don't know. I don't like me when they say I like making quick decisions Like I'll see a time even my father will emphasize something relatively quickly if he trusts the person who's giving him the advice So so much that comes out to trusting you The way you build trust is by getting into this ultra tight rapport and the way you do that Is by asking good questions and then listen to your answers. Uh-huh It's your active listening make sure that and that starts to eat away the limiting beliefs right then and there Now that being said, let's say you've done all that and you're getting out to the point where you're at the end of the sale And you're in your looping patterns, right? This is what happens with action You When you want to attack is I make my best decision slowly right and I don't trust sales people and the way to offset that is number one with guarantees Overing someone a guarantee or a rescission period and then most importantly making them run A different movie you went through this with looping a few weeks ago, you know when you say what's the worst that can possibly happen Let's say I'm wrong and it doesn't work as well and you lose or of course you're 400 dollars and you have to cancel the product It's not going to put me in the poor house now. Will it so do you run your looping patterns? Say but on the upside how do you get a half right? What do views benefit benefit? It's not stacking benzin give me one shot and then if you can you do a step down sale Meaning let's say you're going to sell them a thousand dollars a product try to start let's start smaller Do 500 dollars see if you can do a step down to a smaller starting point Let them get a taste of your product and then do the upsell again Two weeks if the one thing about high action threshold people is that one should got them that resistance Drops almost like as soon as they sign become your profit your customer They're a different breed. No, okay Uh, listen, I will tell you this is probably no better seriously When it comes to attracting the opposite sex the straight line is incredibly effective Because it cuts to the tools of human influence to connect with another human being It's not just to get someone to a lot to a to be attracted to you, but to get them to like you So it's more like also for longer term relationship as well. It's not just about like trying to get laid It's also to actually make someone genuinely fall in love with you and the straight line system allows you to happen So often is that you are an awesome person? You probably are you have all these great qualities You've always so much to offer someone else not just money, but also just you as a person and yet because your Internal communication platform is subpar you can't get that across to someone else The straight line system allows you To be your best self to get your best self across You could be this awesome person and you probably are but you don't know how to Project that out to the world Well by using the straight line system with tonality body language and all the language pads just understanding how you You know, what's the one let's close this one thought What's the one thing that it's almost to a cliche that when a woman doesn't like a guy after their first date What does she say?
